parts.title: The Unfair Rule
parts.text: Ava, Jabari, and Luna served on the Maple Creek Elementary student council. One day, a new rule banned all recess games that used balls. Many students called this an **injustice** because they had done nothing wrong. Jabari said they should review the school's **constitution** — the set of written rules that guided how decisions were made. Luna agreed and suggested they form a **tribunal**, a special group to hear the case fairly.

parts.title: Hearing the Case
parts.text: The next morning, the **defendant** — the student who had accidentally broken a window with a kickball — stood before the council. She gave her **testimony**, explaining exactly what had happened that day. Several classmates also spoke up to **uphold** her story, confirming every detail. Ava reminded everyone that it is wrong to **condemn** all students for one accident.

parts.title: The Decision
parts.text: After careful discussion, the council reached a **verdict**: the ball ban was unfair. Luna wrote an official **decree** announcing that recess games with balls would be allowed again. Jabari made a motion to **abolish** the old rule completely, and the council voted to **repeal** it. Ava added that playing at recess was a **privilege** that came with being responsible and respectful.

parts.title: A Fairer School
parts.text: The next day, students cheered as they ran outside with their kickballs and basketballs. The council had listened, asked questions, and made a fair choice. Ava, Jabari, and Luna felt proud knowing that every voice at Maple Creek mattered.

definitions.meaning1: The final decision made after hearing all the facts. Example: The jury announced its verdict after three hours of discussion.
definitions.studentChallenge: After listening to both sides, the judge gave her ___.

definitions.meaning1: A statement someone makes to tell what they saw or know, especially in a hearing or court. Example: The witness gave her testimony about what she saw at the park.
definitions.studentChallenge: The student gave his ___ about what really happened on the playground.

definitions.meaning1: Something that is very unfair or not right. Example: Making one person do all the work while others do nothing is an injustice.
definitions.studentChallenge: The students spoke up against the ___ of punishing the whole class for one person's mistake.

definitions.meaning1: To say strongly that something is wrong or to blame someone harshly. Example: The principal condemned the bullying and said it would never be allowed.
definitions.studentChallenge: It is not fair to ___ someone before hearing their side of the story.

definitions.meaning1: A person who is accused of doing something wrong and must answer questions about it. Example: The defendant stood before the judge and promised to tell the truth.
definitions.studentChallenge: The ___ waited nervously while the council discussed what had happened.

definitions.meaning1: To support or agree with a decision, rule, or belief. Example: The council voted to uphold the rule that everyone must clean up after lunch.
definitions.studentChallenge: The judges decided to ___ the original decision because it was fair.

definitions.meaning1: A special right or advantage that is given to certain people or earned through good behavior. Example: Staying up late on weekends is a privilege, not a right.
definitions.studentChallenge: Extra recess time is a ___ that students earn by following the rules.

definitions.meaning1: To officially end or get rid of something, such as a law or practice. Example: The country voted to abolish the unfair tax that hurt poor families.
definitions.studentChallenge: The council voted to ___ the old dress code because nobody liked it.

definitions.meaning1: An official order or announcement made by someone in charge. Example: The king issued a decree that all children must attend school.
definitions.meaning2: To officially order something. Example: The mayor decreed that the park would stay open on holidays.
definitions.studentChallenge: The principal issued a ___ that the school carnival would be held on Friday.

definitions.meaning1: To officially cancel or take back a law or rule. Example: The town voted to repeal the rule that banned skateboarding in the park.
definitions.studentChallenge: The student council voted to ___ the no-hats-indoors rule after students complained.

definitions.meaning1: A written set of rules and principles that a group or country follows. Example: The United States Constitution explains how the government should work.
definitions.studentChallenge: Our class wrote a ___ that listed the rights and responsibilities of every student.

definitions.meaning1: A special court or group of people chosen to settle a disagreement or judge a case. Example: The tribunal listened to both sides before making its decision.
definitions.studentChallenge: A ___ of three teachers was formed to decide whether the science fair rules were fair.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: After hearing all the evidence, the jury reached a _______ of not guilty.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The student council announced its _______ — the lunch menu would stay the same.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The witness gave her _______ about what she saw happen at the park that afternoon.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Each student offered _______ describing how the food fight started in the cafeteria.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: Punishing the whole team for one player's mistake felt like a terrible _______.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The book told the story of people who fought against _______ and won their freedom.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The teacher said she would _______ cheating in any form — it was never acceptable.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: It is unfair to _______ someone without first listening to their explanation.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The _______ stood before the council and explained her side of the story.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: In the mock trial, Marcus played the _______ who was accused of stealing the class mascot.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The judges voted to _______ the referee's call because it was the correct one.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Good citizens try to _______ the laws that protect everyone's rights.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: Using the school computers is a _______ that can be taken away if misused.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Earning the _______ of leading the morning announcements made her feel proud.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The leaders voted to _______ the unfair law that kept children out of the library.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Many brave people worked for years to _______ slavery in the United States.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The king issued a _______ that all villagers would receive free bread on market day.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The principal's _______ made it official — pajama day would happen every last Friday.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The town voted to _______ the old parking rule because it no longer made sense.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: When the student council decided to _______ the homework-on-weekends policy, everyone cheered.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The class spent a week writing their own _______ with rules about fairness and respect.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The _______ of the United States begins with the words "We the People."

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: A _______ of three judges was formed to settle the argument between the two teams.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The school set up a _______ to decide whether the playground rules needed to change.

secretPassage.instructions: Read the passage below. Fill in each blank with the correct vocabulary word from the word bank.
secretPassage.passage:
At Riverside Elementary, the student council had a _(1)_ — a written set of rules that everyone agreed to follow. One day, the council passed a rule banning all trading cards at school. Many students thought this was an _(2)_ because trading cards had never caused any problems.

A group of fourth graders asked the council to form a _(3)_ to hear their complaint. The _(4)_ in the case was a boy named Sam, who had been caught trading cards at lunch. Sam gave his _(5)_, explaining that he had not known about the new rule. Three of his friends spoke up to _(6)_ his story, saying he was telling the truth.

Some council members wanted to _(7)_ Sam and take away his recess for a week. But Mia, the council president, reminded them that recess was a _(8)_ every student deserved as long as they followed the rules. After a long discussion, the council reached its _(9)_: Sam would not be punished.

Mia then wrote an official _(10)_ announcing the council's decision to the whole school. The council also voted to _(11)_ the trading-card ban and _(12)_ the rule entirely, so no one else would get in trouble for something that was harmless.

wordScaleImposter.studentPick: Pick 2 imposters and explain why they are opposites of the vocabulary word.
